text: The patient that we're looking at today is a forty-year-old male who presented with bilateral inguinal hernias. The hernias were painless. He noted bilateral inguinal bulges after vigorous activity. He was active in sports. He had had no prior hernia surgery, and he had no gastrointestinal complaints. We discussed different options, including surgery and alternatives. and decided that given his young age and his active lifestyle that a hernia repair was appropriate. I discussed the risks and benefits of open and laparoscopic repairs, and he was eager to have a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair bilaterally.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair by making an incision beneath the umbilicus, dividing the anterior rectus fascia. A balloon dissector is inserted beneath the right rectus muscle and posterior rectus fascia, advanced to the pubic symphysis, and expanded. This is then replaced with a structural balloon cannula. The operation involves dissection of both iliac fossae, reduction of the hernias, and placement of two separate mesh pieces in the preperitoneal space, tacked minimally to the undersurface of the inguinal canals using Cooper's ligament. The patient is supine with three laparoscopic ports: a 10mm structural balloon cannula below the umbilicus and two 5mm cannulae in the mid-hypogastric and suprapubic positions.

The ten millimeter thirty degree laparoscope is positioned in the insufflated preperitoneal space. The surgeon performs blunt dissection to identify anatomical landmarks, including the linea alba. A five millimeter cannula is placed in the suprapubic position, and another in the mid-hypogastric position through the linea alba. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are visible on the right side of the screen. The surgeon carefully dissects to expose the right-sided Cooper's ligament and the iliopubic tract, using gentle sweeping motions to avoid avulsing blood vessels.

The surgeon uses two graspers as blunt dissectors, with one retracting the abdominal wall upward and the other pulling downward. A direct hernia is visible medial to the epigastric vessels, with preperitoneal fat protruding into the direct space. The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid tearing large blood vessels. The iliac vein is identified as the lateralmost structure during dissection along Cooper's ligament, with the iliac artery slightly more lateral. The femoral space is dissected between Cooper's ligament and the iliopubic tract, revealing the iliac vein and a potential small femoral hernia, which is carefully examined.

The surgeon identifies an iliac lymph node just medial to the iliac vein, noting its resistance to traction, and decides to leave it in place. The dissection continues from medial to lateral, removing preperitoneal fat from the dissection area to facilitate mesh placement. The iliopubic tract is visible, with a small direct hernia located just above it. The inferior epigastric artery and vein are seen, marking the boundary between the deep inguinal ring and the direct space.

The surgeon carefully dissects preperitoneal fat from the iliac fossa to prevent mesh crinkling during placement. The iliac vein is visible, and dissection proceeds laterally over the iliac artery, with caution exercised to avoid bleeding from small vessel branches. The edge of the peritoneum is seen adherent to the spermatic cord, with the vas deferens appearing as a bright white structure. The surgeon peels the peritoneum downward, revealing vascular structures running with the fat, while avoiding injury to sensory nerves in the iliac fossa wall.

The surgeon examines the peritoneum adherent to the spermatic cord, noting it does not pass through the deep inguinal ring, indicating it is not a true indirect inguinal hernia. A small direct hernia is visible, with the iliac vein and artery adjacent to the spermatic cord. The surgeon peels down preperitoneal fat to ensure unobstructed mesh placement. The dissection of the right iliac fossa is complete, and the surgeon begins an identical dissection on the left iliac fossa, identifying Cooper's ligament and associated vascular structures while avoiding avulsion of small blood vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the spermatic cord on the left side, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels at the upper part of the screen. Using graspers, the cord is encircled to examine it for any hernia sac or preperitoneal fat. The peritoneum is peeled down from the spermatic cord, showing normal cord structures without significant hernia presence. A sizable piece of preperitoneal fat is gently pulled from the inguinal canal, potentially explaining the patient's symptomatic hernia on the left side.

The surgeon returns to the right side, carefully dissecting near the deep inguinal ring to identify preperitoneal fat. Arterial pulsations are visible through the fat, indicating the proximity of the iliac artery. Upon applying gentle traction, preperitoneal fat is revealed extending into the inguinal canal, similar to the left side. The surgeon peels away the fat to prepare for mesh placement over the deep ring, ensuring the fat does not re-enter the canal. The dissection reveals lymph nodes embedded in the fat, with the iliac artery clearly visible beneath.

The surgeon examines the deep inguinal ring, which is now empty after reducing the preperitoneal fat bilaterally. A six by four inch piece of medium weight polypropylene mesh is introduced into the preperitoneal space beneath the inguinal canal. The mesh is initially turned ninety degrees but is adjusted to ensure the long axis points deep into the pelvis. The mesh is positioned to hang loosely without folding, secured by tacks placed above the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon uses absorbable polyglycolic acid tacks to secure the polypropylene mesh above the iliopubic tract, avoiding sensory nerves and potential postoperative pain. The mesh is positioned to cover both the direct and indirect hernia spaces, with tacks placed into Cooper's ligament. The surgeon maneuvers the mesh to ensure it overlaps the hernia defect, applying counter pressure to the abdominal wall to stabilize the mesh. The mesh is secured with three tacks along its superior edge, ensuring it hangs without crimping.
